summary: Charity Majors explains how to identify your team's critical path by asking 'what makes you money?' — the essential business operations that justify waking someone up at 3am. She argues teams should keep this list compact and explicit, which also clarifies that everything else can wait until morning.
key_points: Your critical path is defined by what makes you money and what your customers care most about
The critical path should be as compact and well-defined as possible
Being explicit about critical path means being explicit that everything else can wait
This is fundamentally a question about service level objectives (SLOs)
Resist the urge to page on every service deterioration — not everything is worth waking someone up for
money_quotes: Like, if you could only deploy three end-to-end checks that would perform entire operations on your site and ensure they work at all times, what would they be?

The idea here is to draw up a list of the things that are absolutely worth waking someone up to fix immediately, night or day, rain or shine. That list should be as compact and well-defined as possible.
This allows you to be explicit about the fact that anything else can wait til morning, or some other less-demanding service level agreement.

Someone will usually seize this opportunity to argue that absolutely any deterioration in service is worth paging someone immediately to fix it, day or night. They are wrong, but it's good to flush these assumptions out and have this argument kindly out in the open.
key_insight: Your critical path is defined by asking 'what makes us money?' — keep the list of page-worthy operations as small as possible so you can explicitly acknowledge that everything else can wait.
